query: A/B test results: Variant A: 42/492 conversions. Variant B: 35/324 conversions. Using Bayesian analysis, what is the probability that B is better than A?
response: Model each variant's conversion rate as Beta distributed. Sample from both posteriors and estimate P(B > A) by Monte Carlo. import numpy as np from scipy.stats import beta # Beta posteriors from uniform prior + data a_alpha = 1 + 42 a_beta = 1 + 492 - 42 b_alpha = 1 + 35 b_beta = 1 + 324 - 35 # Monte Carlo: sample from both, count P(B > A) rng = np.random.default_rng(42) n_samples = 100_000 a_samples = rng.beta(a_alpha, a_beta, n_samples) b_samples = rng.beta(b_alpha, b_beta, n_samples) p_b_better = np.mean(b_samples > a_samples) print(f'A: {a_alpha-1}/{a_alpha+a_beta-2} conversions, mean rate = {a_alpha/(a_alpha+a_beta):.3f}') print(f'B: {b_alpha-1}/{b_alpha+b_beta-2} conversions, mean rate = {b_alpha/(b_alpha+b_beta):.3f}') print(f'P(B better than A): {p_b_better:.3f} = {p_b_better:.1%}') print(f'Recommendation: {"Use B" if p_b_better > 0.95 else "Need more data" if p_b_better > 0.5 else "Keep A"}') A: 42/492 conversions, mean rate = 0.087 B: 35/324 conversions, mean rate = 0.110 P(B better than A): 0.863 = 86.3% Recommendation: Need more data

query: A disease has 1.0% prevalence. A test has 86% sensitivity and 95% specificity. A patient takes 3 independent tests, all positive. Show the posterior probability after each test.
response: After each positive test, use the current posterior as the new prior. This sequential updating is the core of Bayesian inference. sensitivity = 0.86 specificity = 0.95 prior = 0.01 # baseline prevalence def bayes_update(prior, sensitivity, specificity, positive=True): if positive: p_evidence = sensitivity * prior + (1 - specificity) * (1 - prior) return (sensitivity * prior) / p_evidence else: p_evidence = (1-sensitivity) * prior + specificity * (1 - prior) return ((1-sensitivity) * prior) / p_evidence print(f'Prior probability: {prior:.4f} = {prior:.2%}') posterior = prior for i in range(1, 4): posterior = bayes_update(posterior, sensitivity, specificity, positive=True) print(f'After test {i} (positive): {posterior:.4f} = {posterior:.2%}') Prior probability: 0.0100 = 1.00% After test 1 (positive): 0.1480 = 14.80% After test 2 (positive): 0.7493 = 74.93% After test 3 (positive): 0.9809 = 98.09% Summarize the final posterior and quantify the belief update. print(f'\nFinal posterior after 3 positive tests: {posterior:.4f} = {posterior:.2%}') print(f'Odds ratio increase: {posterior/(1-posterior) / (0.01/(1-0.01)):.1f}x') Final posterior after 3 positive tests: 0.9809 = 98.09% Odds ratio increase: 5088.4x

Likelihood: Binomial. Posterior: Beta(1+H, 1+T). from scipy.stats import beta import numpy as np heads = 11 tails = 3 # Prior: Beta(1,1) = Uniform alpha_prior, beta_prior = 1, 1 # Posterior: Beta(alpha + heads, beta + tails) alpha_post = alpha_prior + heads beta_post = beta_prior + tails posterior = beta(alpha_post, beta_post) mean = posterior.mean() ci_low, ci_high = posterior.ppf(0.025), posterior.ppf(0.975) print(f'Observations: {heads} heads, {tails} tails') print(f'Posterior: Beta({alpha_post}, {beta_post})') print(f'Posterior mean (estimated bias): {mean:.3f}') print(f'95% credible interval: [{ci_low:.3f}, {ci_high:.3f}]') Observations: 11 heads, 3 tails Posterior: Beta(12, 4) Posterior mean (estimated bias): 0.750 95% credible interval: [0.519, 0.922]

query: A disease has 5.0% prevalence. A test has 97% sensitivity and 88% specificity. A patient takes 3 independent tests, all positive. Show the posterior probability after each test.
response: After each positive test, use the current posterior as the new prior. This sequential updating is the core of Bayesian inference. sensitivity = 0.97 specificity = 0.88 prior = 0.05 # baseline prevalence def bayes_update(prior, sensitivity, specificity, positive=True): if positive: p_evidence = sensitivity * prior + (1 - specificity) * (1 - prior) return (sensitivity * prior) / p_evidence else: p_evidence = (1-sensitivity) * prior + specificity * (1 - prior) return ((1-sensitivity) * prior) / p_evidence print(f'Prior probability: {prior:.4f} = {prior:.2%}') posterior = prior for i in range(1, 4): posterior = bayes_update(posterior, sensitivity, specificity, positive=True) print(f'After test {i} (positive): {posterior:.4f} = {posterior:.2%}') Prior probability: 0.0500 = 5.00% After test 1 (positive): 0.2985 = 29.85% After test 2 (positive): 0.7747 = 77.47% After test 3 (positive): 0.9653 = 96.53% Summarize the final posterior and quantify the belief update. print(f'\nFinal posterior after 3 positive tests: {posterior:.4f} = {posterior:.2%}') print(f'Odds ratio increase: {posterior/(1-posterior) / (0.05/(1-0.05)):.1f}x') Final posterior after 3 positive tests: 0.9653 = 96.53% Odds ratio increase: 528.2x

Word likelihoods given spam: {'money':0.8,'prize':0.75,'click':0.7,'free':0.85,'win':0.78,'meeting':0.05,'project':0.03,'report':0.04,'lunch':0.06,'schedule':0.04}. Word likelihoods given ham: {'money':0.1,'prize':0.05,'click':0.15,'free':0.12,'win':0.08,'meeting':0.6,'project':0.7,'report':0.65,'lunch':0.55,'schedule':0.5}.
response: Naive Bayes: P(spam|words) ∝ P(spam) × Π P(word|spam). Compare log-probabilities for numerical stability. import math email = ['win', 'free', 'project', 'schedule', 'money'] p_spam_prior = 0.4 p_ham_prior = 0.6 spam_likelihoods = {'money':0.8,'prize':0.75,'click':0.7,'free':0.85,'win':0.78, 'meeting':0.05,'project':0.03,'report':0.04,'lunch':0.06,'schedule':0.04} ham_likelihoods = {'money':0.1,'prize':0.05,'click':0.15,'free':0.12,'win':0.08, 'meeting':0.6,'project':0.7,'report':0.65,'lunch':0.55,'schedule':0.5} log_spam = math.log(p_spam_prior) log_ham = math.log(p_ham_prior) for word in email: if word in spam_likelihoods: log_spam += math.log(spam_likelihoods[word]) log_ham += math.log(ham_likelihoods[word]) print(f' {word}: P(spam)={spam_likelihoods[word]}, P(ham)={ham_likelihoods[word]}') # Normalize log_total = math.log(math.exp(log_spam) + math.exp(log_ham)) p_spam_post = math.exp(log_spam - log_total) p_ham_post = math.exp(log_ham - log_total) print(f'\nP(spam | email): {p_spam_post:.3f}') print(f'P(ham | email): {p_ham_post:.3f}') print(f'Classification: {"SPAM" if p_spam_post > 0.5 else "HAM"}') win: P(spam)=0.78, P(ham)=0.08 free: P(spam)=0.85, P(ham)=0.12 project: P(spam)=0.03, P(ham)=0.7 schedule: P(spam)=0.04, P(ham)=0.5 money: P(spam)=0.8, P(ham)=0.1 P(spam | email): 0.558 P(ham | email): 0.442 Classification: SPAM

response: After each positive test, use the current posterior as the new prior. Compare log-probabilities for numerical stability. import math email = ['free', 'project', 'report', 'money', 'prize'] p_spam_prior = 0.4 p_ham_prior = 0.6 spam_likelihoods = {'money':0.8,'prize':0.75,'click':0.7,'free':0.85,'win':0.78, 'meeting':0.05,'project':0.03,'report':0.04,'lunch':0.06,'schedule':0.04} ham_likelihoods = {'money':0.1,'prize':0.05,'click':0.15,'free':0.12,'win':0.08, 'meeting':0.6,'project':0.7,'report':0.65,'lunch':0.55,'schedule':0.5} log_spam = math.log(p_spam_prior) log_ham = math.log(p_ham_prior) for word in email: if word in spam_likelihoods: log_spam += math.log(spam_likelihoods[word]) log_ham += math.log(ham_likelihoods[word]) print(f' {word}: P(spam)={spam_likelihoods[word]}, P(ham)={ham_likelihoods[word]}') # Normalize log_total = math.log(math.exp(log_spam) + math.exp(log_ham)) p_spam_post = math.exp(log_spam - log_total) p_ham_post = math.exp(log_ham - log_total) print(f'\nP(spam | email): {p_spam_post:.3f}') print(f'P(ham | email): {p_ham_post:.3f}') print(f'Classification: {"SPAM" if p_spam_post > 0.5 else "HAM"}') free: P(spam)=0.85, P(ham)=0.12 project: P(spam)=0.03, P(ham)=0.7 report: P(spam)=0.04, P(ham)=0.65 money: P(spam)=0.8, P(ham)=0.1 prize: P(spam)=0.75, P(ham)=0.05 P(spam | email): 0.599 P(ham | email): 0.401 Classification: SPAM

query: A coin is flipped 13 times, getting 4 heads and 9 tails. Starting with a uniform prior, use Bayesian updating to find the posterior distribution of the coin's bias.
response: With a uniform prior and binomial likelihood, the posterior is Beta(α + heads, β + tails) where α=β=1 for the uniform prior. The posterior mean is (1+heads)/(2+total). Prior: Beta(1,1). Likelihood: Binomial. Posterior: Beta(1+H, 1+T). from scipy.stats import beta import numpy as np heads = 4 tails = 9 # Prior: Beta(1,1) = Uniform alpha_prior, beta_prior = 1, 1 # Posterior: Beta(alpha + heads, beta + tails) alpha_post = alpha_prior + heads beta_post = beta_prior + tails posterior = beta(alpha_post, beta_post) mean = posterior.mean() ci_low, ci_high = posterior.ppf(0.025), posterior.ppf(0.975) print(f'Observations: {heads} heads, {tails} tails') print(f'Posterior: Beta({alpha_post}, {beta_post})') print(f'Posterior mean (estimated bias): {mean:.3f}') print(f'95% credible interval: [{ci_low:.3f}, {ci_high:.3f}]') Observations: 4 heads, 9 tails Posterior: Beta(5, 10) Posterior mean (estimated bias): 0.333 95% credible interval: [0.128, 0.581]

query: A/B test results: Variant A: 33/336 conversions. Variant B: 29/374 conversions. Using Bayesian analysis, what is the probability that B is better than A?
response: Model each variant's conversion rate as Beta distributed. Sample from both posteriors and estimate P(B > A) by Monte Carlo. import numpy as np from scipy.stats import beta # Beta posteriors from uniform prior + data a_alpha = 1 + 33 a_beta = 1 + 336 - 33 b_alpha = 1 + 29 b_beta = 1 + 374 - 29 # Monte Carlo: sample from both, count P(B > A) rng = np.random.default_rng(42) n_samples = 100_000 a_samples = rng.beta(a_alpha, a_beta, n_samples) b_samples = rng.beta(b_alpha, b_beta, n_samples) p_b_better = np.mean(b_samples > a_samples) print(f'A: {a_alpha-1}/{a_alpha+a_beta-2} conversions, mean rate = {a_alpha/(a_alpha+a_beta):.3f}') print(f'B: {b_alpha-1}/{b_alpha+b_beta-2} conversions, mean rate = {b_alpha/(b_alpha+b_beta):.3f}') print(f'P(B better than A): {p_b_better:.3f} = {p_b_better:.1%}') print(f'Recommendation: {"Use B" if p_b_better > 0.95 else "Need more data" if p_b_better > 0.5 else "Keep A"}') A: 33/336 conversions, mean rate = 0.101 B: 29/374 conversions, mean rate = 0.080 P(B better than A): 0.167 = 16.7% Recommendation: Keep A

Posterior: Beta(1+H, 1+T). from scipy.stats import beta import numpy as np heads = 2 tails = 6 # Prior: Beta(1,1) = Uniform alpha_prior, beta_prior = 1, 1 # Posterior: Beta(alpha + heads, beta + tails) alpha_post = alpha_prior + heads beta_post = beta_prior + tails posterior = beta(alpha_post, beta_post) mean = posterior.mean() ci_low, ci_high = posterior.ppf(0.025), posterior.ppf(0.975) print(f'Observations: {heads} heads, {tails} tails') print(f'Posterior: Beta({alpha_post}, {beta_post})') print(f'Posterior mean (estimated bias): {mean:.3f}') print(f'95% credible interval: [{ci_low:.3f}, {ci_high:.3f}]') Observations: 2 heads, 6 tails Posterior: Beta(3, 7) Posterior mean (estimated bias): 0.300 95% credible interval: [0.075, 0.600]

query: A test for hypertension has sensitivity 86% and specificity 80%. The disease affects 15.0% of the population. A patient tests positive. What is the probability they actually have hypertension?
response: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(disease|positive) = P(positive|disease)·P(disease) / P(positive). P(positive) = sensitivity·prevalence + (1-specificity)·(1-prevalence). prevalence = 0.15 # P(disease) sensitivity = 0.86 # P(positive | disease) specificity = 0.8 # P(negative | no disease) # P(positive) = P(pos|disease)*P(disease) + P(pos|no disease)*P(no disease) p_pos = sensitivity * prevalence + (1 - specificity) * (1 - prevalence) # Bayes: P(disease | positive) p_disease_given_pos = (sensitivity * prevalence) / p_pos print(f'P(positive): {p_pos:.4f}') print(f'P(disease | positive test): {p_disease_given_pos:.4f} = {p_disease_given_pos:.1%}') print(f'False positive rate: {1 - p_disease_given_pos:.1%}') P(positive): 0.2990 P(disease | positive test): 0.4314 = 43.1% False positive rate: 56.9%
